---
title: "Applied — A Neural Network in PyTorch"
engine: markdown
---

::: {.lm-hero}
[Chapter 9 · Neural Networks]{.eyebrow}

# Applied — A Neural Network in PyTorch

[Build and train a small network on Fashion-MNIST, and meet the training loop you will reuse, unchanged in shape, all the way to the capstone.]{.dek}
:::

This is the first rung toward the book's capstone. Here you build and train a small feedforward network in PyTorch on a real image dataset, [Fashion-MNIST]{.term}, with a complete training loop. That loop is the whole point. Draw a minibatch, compute the loss, backpropagate, step the optimizer: the same loop, unchanged in shape, trains the convolutional network of Chapter 10 and the GPT of Chapter 12. Learn it once, here.

## The data

Fashion-MNIST is 70,000 grayscale images, 28&times;28 pixels, each a garment in one of ten classes. We flatten every image to a 784-vector, scale the pixels to [0,1], and work with a random subsample so training finishes in seconds on a CPU. An 80/20 split, stratified by class, gives held-out test images to judge generalization.

## The model

A plain feedforward network: two hidden layers with [ReLU]{.term} and a little [dropout]{.term}, then a linear layer to ten class scores. The `forward` method returns the raw scores, the [logits]{.term}; cross-entropy applies the softmax internally. It is the loss of logistic regression from Chapter 4, generalized from two classes to ten.

::: {.defbox}
[The Training Loop]{.lbl}

[ for each minibatch: &nbsp; logits &rarr; loss &rarr; `zero_grad` &rarr; `backward` &rarr; `step` ]{.math}
:::

From Chapter 9 onward the models are trained with PyTorch, which needs a numerical stack and ideally a GPU. That is more than a browser sandbox can carry, so this chapter runs in **Google Colab**: one click opens the notebook on Google's free hardware, no local install.

```{=html}
<a class="colab-btn" href="https://colab.research.google.com/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span class="dot"></span> Open in Google Colab</a>
```

The model is a stack of affine maps and nonlinearities, defined once and moved to the available device. The code below is here for reference; open it in Colab to run and modify it.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(784, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.2),
            nn.Linear(256, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.2),
            nn.Linear(128, 10),                 # ten class scores
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)        # logits; CrossEntropyLoss applies the softmax

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model = MLP().to(device)
```

The training loop is the heart of the notebook. Each epoch reshuffles the training set and walks it in minibatches; each batch zeroes the gradients, computes the cross-entropy loss, backpropagates, and steps the optimizer. Track the held-out accuracy as you go.

```python
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
batch_size, epochs = 128, 12

for epoch in range(epochs):
    order = torch.randperm(len(X_tr))            # reshuffle each epoch
    for i in range(0, len(X_tr), batch_size):
        batch = order[i : i + batch_size]
        logits = model(X_tr[batch])              # forward pass
        loss = loss_fn(logits, y_tr[batch])      # cross-entropy
        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        loss.backward()                          # backpropagation
        optimizer.step()                         # theta <- theta - lr * grad
```

A couple of seconds of training reaches the high-80s in test accuracy. The mistakes are revealing: shirts, coats, and pullovers are genuinely hard to tell apart at 28&times;28. The network treats the 784 pixels as an unordered list, with no notion that two pixels are neighbors. Chapter 10 fixes exactly that with a convolutional model, on this same data and with this same loop; only the model changes.

::: {.explore}
[Try it]{.lbl}

In Colab, add a third hidden layer or change the widths and dropout. How far past the high-80s can a plain MLP push on this data? Then keep the loop in mind: in Chapter 12 it trains a GPT, where the data and model differ but `zero_grad → backward → step` is identical. The capstone is this notebook, scaled up.
:::
